title: practical english usage - Part 167
content:
I'm thinking of studying medicine.

think of/about (     think to) I don't want to take part in any more conferences.

take part in (     at     eŋ Everybody was surprised at/by the weather.

My wife is suffering from hepatitis.

Could I speak to/with your father for a moment?

speak to; speak with (especially AmE) I feel really sorry for her children.

sorry for a person (sympathy) I'm sorry for/about breaking your window.

sorry for/about something that one has done (apologising) I'm sorry about your exam results.

sorry about something that has happened (not the speaker's fault) If you smile at me like that, I'll give you anything you want.

grammar •  214   expressions without prepositions How long are you staying (for)?

I've been here (for) three weeks now.

something lasts.

In an informal style,/or is often left out in expressions that say how  long 7 'how   long' I'll see you (at) about three o'clock.

In an informal style, at is often dropped before about + time expression.

Which day do you have your music lesson?

What day is your hair appointment?

In an informal style, we  can also leave out on before what/which day(s).

What time does Granny's train arrive? (More natural than At what time...?) We   usually leave out at before what time.

5   What      time     .. etc For per in expressions like these, ►    322.2.

Private lessons cost £20  an hour.

eighty pence a kilo.

No preposition is used in expressions like three times a day, sixty miles an hour, 4 a  meaning       'each' Why don't you come for a drink (on) Monday evening?

the week.

In an informal style, we  sometimes leave out on before the names of the days of 3 days    of  the   week Note also tomorrow morning, yesterday afternoon, etc.

Come any day you like.  The party lasted all night.

I'll never forget meeting you that afternoon.

The meeting's this Thursday. We met one Tuesday in August.

See you next Monday, (     ... on next Monday.) informal style), all.

beginning next, last, this, that (sometimes), one, every, each, some, any (in an Prepositions are not used before a number of common expressions of time 2 next,     last,    etc resemblance to  approach to  marriage to discussion of   emphasis on   entry into   experience of Note that the related nouns must have prepositions before complements: not The train is now approaching Paddington, (     .. .-approaching to. ..) not The child does not resemble either of its parents, (     ... resemble to ...) not He's clever, but he lacks experience, (     .. .-lactes-ef...) not She married a friend of her sister's, (     ... -married with ...) the church;) not Conversation stopped as we entered the church, (     ... entered in(to) grammar •  214   expressions without prepositions We need a place to live (in). She had no place to go (to).

This is particularly common with the noun place.

We have an hour to do it (in).

She has no money to buy food (with).

preposition (►   102.5).

Prepositions can sometimes be dropped in the structure noun + infinitive + 12 infinitive       structures I always said you'd go places. (= become successful) Let's go (to) some place where it's quiet.

This is normal in American English.

In an informal style, to can be dropped in some expressions with the word place.

In informal English (especially American), at can be left out before home.

I'm going home.

We   do not use to before home (►  489).

They plant corn (in) the same way their ancestors used to 500 years ago.

way, (in) the same way, (in) another way, etc.

We   often leave out in (especially in informal speech) in expressions like (in) this 9 (in)  this     way,     etc not What colour are her eyes? (     O  f what colour. . .?) What shoe size are you?

I'm the same weight as I was twenty years ago.

She's the same age as me. His head's a funny shape.

He is just the right height to be a police officer.

preposition.

are usually connected to the subject of the clause by the verb be, without a Expressions containing words like height, weight, length, size, shape, age, colour 8  measurement          expressions,        etc   after    be PrepositionsSection  18 grammar •  Section 19   Basic Clause Types more easily, ►    299.
